coredata.dc:title: Efficient tyrosinase nano-inhibitor based on carbon dots behaving as a gathering of hydrophobic cores and key chemical group
coredata.dc:creator: Barros, Marcela Rodrigues
da Silva, Lucas Pereira
Menezes, Thais Meira
Garcia, Yarima Sanchez
Neves, Jorge Luiz
coredata.dc:description: Small organic molecules have been extensively applied to achieve enzymatic inhibition. Although numerous efforts have been made to deliver efficient inhibitors, small inhibitors applications are hindered by many drawbacks. Moreover, reporters comprising nanoparticle inhibitory activity against enzymes are very scarce in the literature. In this scenario, carbon nanodots (CDs) emerge as promising candidates for efficient enzyme inhibition due to their unique properties. Here, CDs specific molecular characteristics (core composition and chemical surface groups) have been investigated to produce a more potent enzyme inhibition. Mushroom tyrosinase (mTyr) has been adopted as an enzymatic prototype. The CDs revealed a high affinity to mTyr (Ka ≈ 106 M−1), mainly through hydrophobic forces and followed by slight mTyr structural alteration. CDs competitively inhibit mTyr, with low inhibition constant (K I = 517.7 ± 17.0 nM), which is up 70 fold smaller then the commercial inhibitor (kojic acid) and the starch nanoparticles previously reported. The results expose that the CDs act as a hydrophobic agglomerate with carboxyl groups on its surface, mimicking characteristics found on small molecule inhibitors (but with superior performance). All these results highlight the CD excellent potential as an efficient low toxic Tyr inhibitor, opening the prospect of using these nanoparticles in the cosmetic and food industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tyrosinase
Carbon dots
Inhibitor
Nanoparticles
Mechanism
Enzymes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism and mTyr affinity to CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The FTIR spectra of CDs showing its functional chemical groups and (b) the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs.
(a) Fluorescence quenching of mTyr by CDs at 308 K and (b) the Stern-Volmer plots.

(a) Modified Stern-Volmer plots at 303, 308 and 313 K. (b) The Van’t Hoff plots for the thermodynamic parameter determination (mTyr-CDs interaction) and (c) the hydrophobic surface of mTyr. The reddish areas represent the high hydrophobic enzyme regions.

(a) CD dose dependent enzymatic activity together with control molecules C1 (2-Acetylfuran) and C2 (4-(2-Furyl)-3-buten-2-one). (b) Lineweaver-Burk plots for the mTyr inhibition at different CD concentration.

(a) Circular dichroism spectra of tyrosinase (5 μM) in the absence (blue) and presence of CDs 1.25 μM (red) and 2.5 μM (green), in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4), at 298 K. (b) The mTyr secondary structure quantification during CD titration.
Thermodynamic parameters for mTyr-CD interaction determined from fluorescence quenching experiments.
CD inhibitory parameter against mTyr in comparison to starch hollow nanoparticles (HNPs), tapioca starch nanoparticles (TSNPs) and Kojic Acid (KA).

head.ce:title: Efficient tyrosinase nano-inhibitor based on carbon dots behaving as a gathering of hydrophobic cores and key chemical group

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Fundamental, CCEN, Universidade Federal de Pernambuco, Recife 50670-901, PE, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Química Fundamental, CCEN, Universidade Federal de Pernambuco

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Química Fundamental, CCEN, Universidade Federal de Pernambuco, Recife 50670-901, PE, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centro de estudos avanzados de Cuba, CEA, Valle Grande, La Lisa 17100, La Habana, Cuba

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de estudos avanzados de Cuba, CEA

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de estudos avanzados de Cuba, CEA, Valle Grande, La Lisa 17100, La Habana, Cuba

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Small organic molecules have been extensively applied to achieve enzymatic inhibition. Although numerous efforts have been made to deliver efficient inhibitors, small inhibitors applications are hindered by many drawbacks. Moreover, reporters comprising nanoparticle inhibitory activity against enzymes are very scarce in the literature. In this scenario, carbon nanodots (CDs) emerge as promising candidates for efficient enzyme inhibition due to their unique properties. Here, CDs specific molecular characteristics (core composition and chemical surface groups) have been investigated to produce a more potent enzyme inhibition. Mushroom tyrosinase (mTyr) has been adopted as an enzymatic prototype. The CDs revealed a high affinity to mTyr (Ka ≈ 106 M−1), mainly through hydrophobic forces and followed by slight mTyr structural alteration. CDs competitively inhibit mTyr, with low inhibition constant (K I = 517.7 ± 17.0 nM), which is up 70 fold smaller then the commercial inhibitor (kojic acid) and the starch nanoparticles previously reported. The results expose that the CDs act as a hydrophobic agglomerate with carboxyl groups on its surface, mimicking characteristics found on small molecule inhibitors (but with superior performance). All these results highlight the CD excellent potential as an efficient low toxic Tyr inhibitor, opening the prospect of using these nanoparticles in the cosmetic and food industries.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes are life-essential biomolecules, promoting a wide range of biochemical processes. Deregulation of these processes is undesirable and can be provoked by enzyme overexpression or hyperactivation, which in turn can lead to several disorders and diseases [1]. For instance, deregulation on diverse melanogenic processes occurs due to an enzyme malfunctioning called Tyrosinase (Tyr). On one side, Tyr overexpression is reported during tumorigenesis and can also lead to skin pigmentation disorders. On the other side, changes in Tyr activity are usually associated with melanoma emergence [2].

Widely found in living organisms, Tyr (EC 1.14.18.1) is an oxidoreductase metalloenzyme also involved in the browning process of fruits and vegetables that occur by damage or due to long-term storage [3,4]. This enzyme has its catalytic center constituted by three histidines coordinated to each of the two type-3 copper nuclei [5] and catalyzes the hydroxylation of monophenols and the subsequent oxidation to the correspondent quinone [6]. Tyrosinase plays a crucial role in melanin synthesis in mammals, acting on the conversion of l-tyrosine/l-DOPA to dopaquinone, starting the melanin synthesis process [7]. There are three enzymes known to be involved in human melanogenesis: Tyrosinase adjusts the amount of pigment produced, and the other two regulate the type of the produced pigment [8,9]. Melanin protects the skin from UV radiation damages, although its overproduction can result in many disorders, like melasma and skin cancer [10,11]. Tyrosinase is also responsible for the biosynthesis of neuromelanin, and its accumulation due to the enzyme overexpression is related to Parkinson's disease [12,13].

Although many small molecule inhibitors have been investigated from natural and synthetic sources [10,8,14,15], molecular inhibitors frequently required high doses to work effectively and present embarrassing low solubility/stability, which raises serious safety concerns [3,16] and leads to hazardous drawbacks for their applications. Most of the enzyme inhibitors have some common moieties (e.g., COOH, OH, and NH and aromatic portions) [17,18], including inhibitor for general metalloenzymes [19] and mushroom Tyr (mTyr) [20–22]. Small molecules produce most frequently non-competitive and mixed inhibition modes for mTyr, which can be explained due to non-specific binding enzyme sites with a specific access channel to the mTyr active site [23]. Our previous investigation [24] exposes the role of specific chemical groups (presented in small furan derivatives) to enhance the mTyr inhibition. The presence of furan oxygenated and hydrophobic groups has been lead to a more efficient Tyr inhibition. Even more effective than commercial options, such as the kojic acid [24]. Besides, several works also report the crucial role of hydrophobic groups to enhance the inhibitory activity toward mTyr [25–28].

As an alternative for those organic enzymatic inhibitors, some works have applied carbon-based nanomaterials to modulate the enzymatic activity [29–32]. Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted attention for their unique and adaptable properties suitable for biological and medical applications [33–36], such as high chemical- and photo-stability, exceptional luminescence, water solubility, and low toxicity. However, when it comes to tyrosinase, works involving CDs-enzyme are customarily addressed to their uses as probes to enzymatic activity sensing and inhibitor screening [37–39,12]. This work aims to explore CDs containing hydrophobic core and carboxyl surface groups and evaluate their inhibitory potential toward the mushroom Tyrosinase (mTyr) activity. By applying fluorescence spectroscopy, we analyzed their interaction with the mTyr to understand the forces involved, and we evaluated the role of physical and chemical properties during the enzyme inhibition.

The mTyr (Agaricus bisporus 1000 U/mg), l-3,4-dihydroxyphenylalanine (l-DOPA), concanavalin A (ConA) from Canavalia ensiformis (Jack bean), the 2-Acetylfuran and 4-(2-Furyl)-3-buten-2-one were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. The reagents NaH2PO4H2O and Na2HPO4·7H2O were obtained from Insofar. All the reagents have an analytical standard. Distilled and deionized water was also used.

The nanoparticles have been obtained by adapting the synthesis already established in the literature [36]. The carbon nanoparticles were obtained by pyrolyzing 30 mg of Concanavalin A (type VI, Sigma). The protein concanavalin A was placed in a furnace at 300 °C for 3 h and cooled to room temperature. Then 3 mL of 70% ethanol was added to extract the nanoparticles. The mixture was sonicated in an Ultrasonic bath (LimpSonic) for 30 min. The resulting solutions were centrifuged twice at 15,000 rpm for 30 min, and the supernatant was extracted. For purification purposes, the nanoparticles were left in an oven at 100 °C and later, the carbon dots were removed in water. The samples were placed in Amicon Ultra-0.5 10 K (Millipore) ultrafiltration devices with a 500 μL volumetric capacity. The filters were placed into collector tubes and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, yielding 500 μl of concentrated carbon dot samples at each centrifugation. The obtained CDs are homogeneous distributed (Fig. S1a), composed primarily of 76% carbon and 15% oxygen (Fig. S1b) and have an average size of ≈5 nm (Fig. S1c). Additionally, the nanoparticles show low crystallinity and are anionic (ζ =−28.2 ± 1.8 mV) [36].

The mTyr stock solution was prepared at 1.5 μM concentration in 20 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6.2), previously dissolved in distilled water. A 6 mM l-DOPA solution was also prepared. Stock solutions of CDs were prepared in distilled water at 5 mg/mL concentration. All stock solutions were kept at 5 °C before the experiments. The 2-Acetylfuran and 4-(2-Furyl)-3-buten-2-one were dissolved in distilled water with a final concentration of 2.4 μM.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the CDs were done on an IR tracer-100 Shimadzu infrared spectrophotometer using KBr pellets as the sample matrix, frequency range 500–4000 cm−1. In addition, the light absorption spectrum (UV–vis) of the CDS was recorded on a UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Erkin Elmer-lambda 650) using a 1 cm cuvette.

The circular dichroism spectra of mTyr (5 μM) during titration with CDs (1.37 μM and 2.5 μM) was carried out at 25 °C utilizing a spectropolarimeter Chirascan (Applied Photophysics, London, UK) working with a 0.1 mm cuvette, under nitrogen atmosphere, in the range of 185–250 nm and performing three scans per sample. The baseline measurement was performed and subtracted from the data (adopting the buffer as a reference). The DichroWeb [40] site was used to analyze the percentage composition of α-helical, β-strands, turns, and unordered secondary structures. For the analysis, the CDSSTR method and protein reference set 6 have been applied. All the experimental and reconstructed data generated by the DichroWeb server are summarized in Fig. S2c and d.

The fluorescence emission spectra were measured on a Jasco FP-6300 spectrofluorometer at 303, 308, and 313 K. The excitation wavelength was chosen to excite the aromatic protein residues (at 280 nm). The emission was monitored between 310 nm and 400 nm. The CDs were titrated to a 201 nM mTyr solution with 0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 60, 80, 100, and 110 μL. Due the high CD luminescence, fluorescence emission of the CDs has been subtracted from the protein spectra prior the analysis and the inner-filter effect has been corrected using the equation [41]
F corr
=
F obs
×
10
A 1
+
A 2
2
, where F
corr is the corrected and F
obs is the observed fluorescence intensity. A 1 and A 2 are the CD absorbances at protein excitation and emission wavelength, respectively.

The Stern-Volmer constant (K SV ) was determined by using the following equation [42]
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the protein fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of MnCQD, respectively. [Q] is the CD concentration, and the protein-CD affinities were determined by fitting the experimental data to the modified Stern-Volmer equation
(2)
log
F 0
− F
F
= log
K a
+ n log [ Q ]
K a is the association constant, n is the number of binding sites.

To calculate the thermodynamic parameter involved in the interaction of mTyr-CDs, we used the Van’t Hoff equation
(3)
lnK a
=
−
Δ H
R
1 T
+
Δ S
R
where K a is the association constant at each temperature; R is the gas constant (8.314 Jmol−1 K−1), T is the temperature in Kelvin, ΔH is the enthalpy variation, and ΔS is the entropy variation. Thus, the graph LnK
a versus 1/T has its angular coefficient (−ΔH/R) and the linear coefficient (ΔS/R).

The enzymatic assays were adapted from literature [6]. In a 96-well microplate, 25 nM mTyr (final concentration) was added in each well (maximum capacity of 300 μL) together with 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40 μL of CD. After 1 h, four different concentrations of l-DOPA were added: 0.25, 0.50, 1.00 and 2.00 mM. Inhibition was monitored by dopachrome product absorption (at 475 nm) for 3.6 min and using an ELISA microplate reader (Bio-rad). All experiments were conducted in triplicate. The enzymatic activity was calculated using the following equation:
(4)
EA =
OD 1
OD 0
× 100
where OD1 is the slope of the reaction kinetics equation with the inhibitor; OD0 is the slope of the kinetic equation in the inhibitor absence. To determine the inhibition type, we fitted the experimental data to the adapted Michaelis-Menten equation below:
(5)
1
v 0
=
K M app
V m app
1
[ S ]
+
1
V m app
where
v 0
is the reaction velocity,
V m app
=
V m
×
1
( 1 + [ I ] /
K IS
)
is the apparent maximum enzyme velocity,
K M app
=
K M
×
( 1 + [ I ] /
K I
)
( 1 + [ I ] /
K IS
)
is the apparent Michaelis-Menten constant. Additionally, [I] is the inhibitor concentration of the inhibitor, K
I is the binding constant of the inhibitor with the free enzyme and K
IS is the binding constant for the inhibitor with enzyme-substrate complex.

Hydrophobic potential surfaces are helpful to understand interactions between the protein and the ligand. The X-ray crystal structure of Agaricus bisporus Tyrosinase (PDBID:2Y9X) was downloaded from the Protein Data Bank (http://www.rcsb.org/pdb) [43]. The hydrophobic surface was calculated by running the script found at (https://pymolwiki.org/index.php/Color-h) on the PyMOL software.

Spectroscopic properties of the CDs have been explored by recording FTIR (Fig. 1 a) and UV–vis (Fig. 1a) spectra. The FTIR spectra show absorption bands around 3350 cm−1 (referring to OH stretching vibrations), the C=O stretching band around 1630 cm−1, and the bands present at 1100 cm−1 referring to the CO stretching. The observed bands evidence oxygenated functional groups, such as carboxylic acid and hydroxyl groups and confer anionic characteristics to the CDs. Besides, (C≡C) stretching vibration is found at 1110 cm−1, and two absorptions bands at 2930–1470 cm−1 (referring to the C-H stretching and C-H folding, respectively) are related to the hydrophobic portion of the CDs [44]. Almost all chemical groups are also found in the starting material ConA (see Fig. S2a), being the main spectral differences the absence of the protein amide II band (NH at 1530 cm−1) and the appearance of two bands (C-O stretching around 1100 cm−1). Due to their oxygenated groups, these CDs show high solubility in water, potentially interacting with biomolecules in solution.

The photoluminescence emission spectra were recorded at room temperature and showed typical fluorescence profile (Fig. S1d), where the emission spectra are red-shifted and change intensities as the absorption wavelength increases. This spectral behavior is compatible with several previous reports of CDs [45,46]. The mechanism accountable for CD fluorescence is not still fully understood. Some ideas have been proposed to explain the fluorescence of these nanoparticles [47]. For example, some authors propose a CQD fluorescence mechanism based on quantum confinement and radiative excitement recombination, which are related to the fact that CQDs have size-dependent emissions [48]. However, the involvement of surface entrapment in their electronic transition has received enormous attention and greater acceptance by researchers. The UV–vis spectrum (Fig. 1b) shows a 230 nm peak, which is ascribed to the π − π* transition [44], and a peak around 250–290 nm ascribed to the π − π* transition of an aromatic system or the n − π* transition of a carbonyl group [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism and mTyr affinity to CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy is a valuable tool for protein studies due to intrinsic fluorescence, and it is also sensitive to protein dynamics [33]. To investigate the interaction of mTyr-CDs, we performed fluorescence quenching experiments. The CD titration has quenched mTyr fluorescence at 308 K (Fig. 2 a), which suggests a binding between the CDs and the enzyme [50]. Quenching experiments at 303 and 313 K can be seen in the supplementary Fig. S3. Besides, a slightly blue shift during CD titration is observed (≈3 nm). It can be related to a decrease in the microenvironment polarity (relative to the aromatic residue vicinities) or changes in the mobile environment [51].

Quenching can occur via different mechanisms, generalized as dynamic, static or a mixture of both mechanisms. One approach to distinguish these mechanisms consists of exploring the temperature dependence of K SV . For static quenching, K SV decreases with increasing temperature, while the opposite occurs in dynamic quenching [50,52]. Fig. 2b shows the Stern-Volmer plots for the Tyrosinase-CDs interaction. The determined K SV values for the three temperatures are summarized in Table 1 , which reveals a dynamic quenching mechanism (since K SV values increase as the temperature increases) for CD-mTyr interaction.

In order to determine the CD enzyme affinity, the experimental fluorescence intensities are fitted to Eq. (2), and the results are displayed in the Fig. 3 a. From these plots, it was possible to determine the association constants (K a ) for the three temperatures (Table 1). Table analysis reveals that mTyr binds tightly to CDs (K a ≈ 106 M−1), and the values are in agreement with previous reported mTyr-nanoparticle interaction [53–55]. Starch nanoparticles (see Table 2 ) have similar superficial groups that can interact with the protein surface (such as CH, CO, and OH) and present similar K a values when compared with the synthesized CDs.

To evaluate thermodynamic parameters and the nature of the Tyrosinase-CDs interaction, we fitted the determined K a to Eq. (3) and the results are presented in the Fig. 3b. These interactions can occur by electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic forces, hydrogen bonds and van der Waals interactions, according to the determined ΔH and ΔS values [52]. As shown in Table 1, the value of ΔG is negative, indicating a spontaneous interaction. The positive value of ΔH and ΔS suggests that hydrophobic interactions drive the Tyrosinase-CD complex formation [56]. The hydrophobic surface of tyrosinase shows several areas with high hydrophobic groups where this interaction can occur, such as the active site surroundings (Fig. 3c).

Since CDs are primarily composed of carbon, they are expected to interact with mTyr by hydrophobic forces, which are found in most protein-nanoparticle interactions. However, as the CDs presented here are anionic, the electrostatic interactions cannot be overlooked [33]. As a general rule, when nanomaterials interact with enzymes cause structural changes, leading to the boost or inhibition of its catalytic activity. For example, the secondary structure of α-chymotrypsin changed due to the adsorption of carbon nanotubes to the enzymatic surface, leading to enzymatic inhibition [31]. The enzyme deformation can also facilitate the CD approaching hydrophobic regions in the active site surrounding and other hydrophobic areas.

On one side, a critical requirement for an inhibitor to behave competitively is related to the aromatic molecular portion (benzene, furan, pyridine, Imidazole, etc.) [57], which confers a certain hydrophobicity to molecules. On another side, the carboxylic acid functional group in their structures seems to play a vital role in a competitive inhibitor structure [58]. Although a precise explanation has not been provided, the ability of the carboxylic acids to chelate the protein copper ions seems to play a crucial role in the mTyr inhibition. Based on this information, the enzymatic assay was performed with CDs and also with two furanosidic molecules (previously reported as mTyr inhibitors [24] and used as control molecules), both containing hydrophobic furanosidic ring and lacking carboxyl groups. The CDs showed an inhibitory effect against the tyrosinase (Fig. 4 a), with IC50 reaching nanomolar doses while the control molecules (C1, C2) do not show such effectivity. It gives us the leads that not only oxygenated but also the presence of carboxyl units can make some difference during inhibition, as reported in several works [6,21]. Studies with carboxylated carbon nanotubes (CNTs) revealed that competitive inhibition towards the α-chymotrypsin activity was observed in carbon nanotubes with a high density of carboxyl groups, and the non-competitive behavior was related to the low carboxyl density [31]. Additionally, mTyr Lineweaver plots (Fig. 4b) reveals that CDs inhibit the enzyme competitively since all lines cross the velocity axis at the same point [59]. More details about the enzymatic assay can be found in supplementary Fig. S4 and S5.

The determined K I and K IS are presented in Table 2, along with kojic acid and previously mTyr nanoparticle inhibitors. Habitually, IC50 (defined as the half-maximal inhibitory concentration) are applied to evaluate the inhibitor performance. Notwithstanding, IC50 values are conditioned to enzyme, inhibitor and substrate concentrations, besides other experimental conditions. As an intrinsic thermodynamic quantity, K I values do not depend on the concentration of chemical species. Thus, inhibitor performance comparisons can be more easily performed [60]. The lower is the K I values more compelling is the competitive inhibitor. CDs have affinity to the free mTyr (K I = 517.7 nM) circa 70 fold than commercial kojic acid (K I = 36,640 nM) or tapioca starch nanoparticles (K I = 35,548 nM). CDs outperformed K I = 8944 nM of the hollow starch nanoparticles (HNPs) by 17 fold. Since the CDs have a carbon core, the hydrophobic interactions between mTyr and the small CD diameter allow it to approximate to mTyr active site, a hydrophobic and highly positive charged area of the enzyme. A fraction of electrostatic interaction could be present due to the anionic CD characteristic (−28.2 mV), and the hydrophobic interaction, resulting in a competitive inhibitor type.

Besides starch nanoparticles, we could not find reports on carbon nanomaterials as tyrosinase inhibitors. Nevertheless, the enzyme interaction with nanomaterials usually leads to structural changes, inhibiting or enhancing the enzyme activity. It is essential to highlight that many carbon nanomaterials can induce conformational changes in proteins (changing the accessibility of the substrates to the active site). The degree of change depends on these nanomaterial types (graphene, C60, carbon nanotube, carbon dots, etc.) and features (shape, size, curvature, dosage, etc.) [61]. For instance, CDs were explored as enzymatic inhibitors of other enzymes [29,30]; Three graphene-based nanomaterials were tested against the horseradish peroxidase enzyme [62], and the graphene and graphene oxide (GO) were reported to reduce the stability and activity of this enzyme through adsorption. In contrast, the reduced graphene oxide (RGO) tuned the enzyme activity by increasing its stability. Yang et al. [63] also reported these contrasting effects between GO and RGO on the activity of cytochrome c peroxidase (cyt c), but this time the RGO inhibited the enzyme. In this case, the cyt c was adsorbed on the RGO by hydrophobic interaction, making it difficult for the enzyme to interact with its substrate and block the active site. Many reports are exploring the effects caused by the interaction between graphene oxide and some enzymes, such as glucose oxidase and l-lactate dehydrogenase [64], α-chymotrypsin [65], and so on.

The circular dichroism is widely used to examine protein secondary structure changes. It makes the technique a valuable tool to identify conformational changes and estimate the changes quantitatively during ligand attachment to the protein structure [66]. Fig. 5 a shows the CD spectra for mTyr alone and in the presence of the CDs. The spectra have two broad bands (one at ≈208 and 222 nm) characteristic of α-helical structure, and another at 215–217 nm, which are also related to the contribution of β-sheet aminoacids [67]. The analysis further revealed (Fig. 5b) that the tyrosinase was primarily composed of circa 51% of α-helical, 20% β-sheet, 11% turns, and 18% of a random coil. The interaction with the CDs caused structural changes by slightly decreasing the helical portion by 6% and increasing in 2% the β-sheet and random coil part; meanwhile, the turns content remained almost the same.

Control over surface-driven modifications to the protein secondary structures is mainly guided by protein biochemical features and intrinsic structural plasticity. Drug molecules explore both features to form binary or even ternary complexes with plasmatic and non-plasmatic protein [68]. Additional features have to be examined when nanomaterials interact with flexible proteins. For instance, nanomaterials morphology may also contribute to protein structural modifications. Rounded nanomaterial surfaces allow remarkable adaptability and extra surface area to attach protein (in some cases, disturb its secondary structure irreversibly) [69] compared to planar surfaces. Innumerable researches rely on physicochemical aspects of protein interactions with many nanomaterials [70]. BSA(Bovine Serum Albumin) structural disturbance caused by Au-NPs [71], ZnO-NPs [72] has been reported, while the same protein maintains structurally intact when absorbed in C60 fullerene NP [73] surface. Moreover, TiO2-NPs induce tubulin conformational changes and reduction in the polymerization [74], while HTF (Human Transferrin) experiences immutable conformational change by interaction with SPIONs [75]. In this regard, all CD features (e.g., size, shape, charges, superficial groups) are responsible together for the different effects on the enzymatic activity [76].

Enzymatic inhibition is an effective way to control the undesirable behavior of enzyme deregulation and its consequences. To overcome small molecular inhibitor downsides, we proposed CDs as an exciting alternative. CDs inhibited the mTyr activity and were bound very firmly to the enzyme mainly by hydrophobic interaction. The results confirmed that creating hydrophobic assemblies with its carboxyl surface groups played a significant role in tyrosinase inhibition, taking the nanoparticle IC50 to nM range (691.30 nM) competitively. Furthermore, CDs exhibited more extraordinary inhibitory performance than the kojic acid and starch nanoparticles previously reported. These strategies used on the CDs can be replicable for other enzymes and paves the way for biocompatible and more efficient inhibition, mitigating the inhibitor drawbacks based on small molecules.

JLN and MTT conceived and designed the research. MJRB and LPSS performed the experiments. YSG performed the CD synthesis and characterizations. MJRB and TMM drafted the manuscript. All authors revised and approved the final version of the manuscript.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the reported work here.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to CNPq (Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico, Brazil) for financial support (Grant number: 420902/2016-3), CAPES (Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior, Brazil), FACEPE (Grant APQ-0459-1.06/2015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2021.112006.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel plant-derived biomedical agents and their biosynthetic origin
Studies in Natural Products Chemistry, vol. 38

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ata A., Novel plant-derived biomedical agents and their biosynthetic origin, 2012, Elsevier, Studies in Natural Products Chemistry, vol. 38, 225–245.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Are human tyrosinase and related proteins suitable targets for melanoma therapy?

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel hydroxypyridinone derivatives containing an oxime ether moiety: synthesis, inhibition on mushroom tyrosinase and application in anti-browning of fresh-cut apples
